summary.overall: Chapter 6 was reviewed in 9 bounded source-grounded shards. Proposed changes: 138 replacements, 0 splits, and 6 removals.
patterns: Paragraph-location scaffolding: paragraph numbers embedded in stems, explanations, and para_context fields (present in approximately 75% of items) teach document trivia rather than operational knowledge.
Thin explanations: many explanations restate the answer or identify a paragraph number without teaching the condition, responsibility, or decision principle underlying the rule.
Redundant T/F+MC pairs: each of four paragraph sections contained a companion T/F question that tested the identical rule as the MC question without adding retrieval value.
Over-concentration on single-sentence paragraphs: 6-1-1 (one sentence) carried seven items; three check activities on the same rule statement were materially duplicative.
Strength: 6-1-4 wake turbulence table coverage spans memory recall, responsibility identification, scenario application, and figure interpretation — a model of cross-format reinforcement.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect, appearing in 15 of 17 replaced items via 'under 6-X-Y' stems or 'Per 6-X-Yz' explanations.
Two true/false items embed long, verbatim source quotations that function as format cues — the specificity guarantees the statement is true.
Distractors in most multiple-choice items are operationally plausible, testing confusion between closely related minima (e.g., 2 vs. 3 minutes, same-course vs. diverging-course distances).
The shard has no source-fidelity errors; all tested facts are correctly drawn from the source blocks.
Cross-format coverage for 6-1-5 terminal minima is thin: only three of the six terminal category pairs are tested, and the en route parallel/crossing runway table is entirely untested.
The 6-2-1 helipad/vertipad reference method and the 6-2-2 mixed DME/ATD qualification are important operational details that warrant multiple-choice formats rather than true/false recognition.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: 18 of 22 items embed paragraph numbers ('Under 6-3-1a,' 'Per 6-4-2c1,' etc.) in stems, explanations, para_context, or flashcard fronts. These teach document navigation trivia rather than operational decision-making.

Thin explanations: Several explanations (particularly 2520ee94: 'That method appears in 6-4-1b') restate an answer or cite a paragraph without teaching the condition, responsibility, or decision principle.
Paragraph references in T/F stems: Three true_false questions embed paragraph references that are irrelevant to verifying the operational rule statement.

Strength: 6-4-1 readback_check (33ac3ba7) and spot_the_error (45d565ec) activities provide complementary phraseology practice — one tests recognition of correct readback, the other tests detection of an incorrect term. Both are well-constructed.

Strength: The four flag-free items (09f22a74 minima_rule_check, ce61c525 minima_rule_check, 81ce83ed flashcard, eacab5da flashcard) demonstrate self-contained stems, operationally relevant content, and proper format fit.
Automated flag inaccuracy: The negative_stem flag was applied to four items (a16b2ff7, 47296efe, 45d565ec, ce61c525) none of which actually use a negative stem construction.

Thin T/F stems: Three true_false questions (c5244557, e3221eeb, 08ba7488) are direct rule restatements with paragraph references prefixed. The operational content is correct but the format adds little beyond the companion multiple-choice items covering the same rules.

Thin explanations in 6-4-5 items: Both the MC question (52a2990b) and T/F question (08ba7488) in 6-4-5 have explanations that merely restate the advisory without teaching the reason — even though the source note block provides that reason (ATD slant-range correction mismatch).

Strength: The minima_rule_check activity ddb0aa8f (6-4-3) and the flashcards 92b083ae (6-4-4) and d21e7eeb (6-4-5) are self-contained, operationally focused, and properly formatted with no paragraph references.

Strength: The readback_check (73eed1eb) and spot_the_error (d3f97fb2) activities for 6-4-4 form a complementary phraseology pair — one tests recognition of correct readback, the other tests detection of an incorrect term in the clearance.

Automated flag inaccuracy: The negative_stem flag was applied to d3f97fb2 (spot_the_error — inherently asks to find wrong word), 27ea7a6b (situation_action asking 'What should you do?'), and 6ab786f4 (spot_the_error) — none use a negative stem construction.

Activities and flashcards are consistently self-contained and free of paragraph-location scaffolding in their student-facing content.

Paragraph 6-7-5 wake turbulence interval items are generally well-constructed; minimal intervention needed.

generation_guidance: Strip all paragraph-number references from stems, explanations, and activity context fields. Students need operational knowledge, not document navigation trivia.

Write explanations that teach the controlling condition, responsibility, or decision rule. When the source states a requirement, threshold, or prohibition, explain what the controller must do and why the operational boundary exists — do not merely repeat the answer or cite a paragraph number.
One MC question per rule is sufficient. Do not generate a companion T/F question that tests the same fact with lower retrieval demand.
Match activity count to source substance: single-sentence paragraphs need at most one check activity and one scenario activity; complex tables support more items across distinct retrieval modes.

When generating visual_interpretation activities, ensure the tested value differs from questions that test the same table from memory, or at minimum the figure activity should require interpreting the visual element (orientation, axis, legend) rather than only reading a text-equivalent value from it.

Prefer situation_action activities that require the student to apply the rule to a realistic operational scenario over directive_check or requirement_check activities that only ask whether a restated sentence is correct.
Remove all paragraph-number references from new item text, explanations, and contextual fields; reference the operational rule directly.
For true/false items on specific operational details (helipad reference method, DME/ATD qualification), use multiple-choice with operationally plausible distractors instead.
Balance choice lengths in knowledge-check items so the correct answer does not stand out by character count or specificity.
Figure-interpretation activities should ask the student to extract a specific value from the figure, not to identify what the figure depicts.
Expand coverage of 6-1-5 terminal minima to test additional category pairs that are currently untested (e.g., Cat B behind Cat B/D, Cat E/F/G behind Cat C).
Add en route parallel/crossing runway items to test the 6-1-5c sub-table, which has no current coverage in this shard.
For situation-action activities, include enough operational context in the situation field that the para_context field is supplementary rather than the primary source of the answer.
Avoid using the word 'under' before a rule description in stems — write the rule condition directly as part of the operational scenario.
Strip all paragraph-number references from stems, explanations, activity context fields, and flashcard fronts. Operational knowledge does not require document navigation citations.

Write explanations that teach the controlling condition, exception, or decision rule. When the source states a threshold (4 miles, 3 minutes, 44 knots), explain what the controller must verify and why the boundary matters operationally — do not restate the answer or cite a paragraph number.

For phraseology items, explanations should clarify the operational difference between similar-sounding phraseology (e.g., 'at or before' vs 'at or after') rather than simply identifying the source paragraph.

Match activity type to the skill: spot_the_error and readback_check for phraseology precision, minima_rule_check for threshold recall, situation_action for contextual decision-making, knowledge_check for rule classification.

Avoid generating true_false items that merely restate a source sentence verbatim with paragraph references. T/F items should test comprehension of the operational principle, not memorization of which subparagraph says what.

Keep minima_rule_check activities self-contained as demonstrated by 09f22a74 and ce61c525: include lookup_context that establishes the operational conditions, and ask for the specific numeric minimum that applies.
Strip all paragraph-number references from stems, explanations, activity para_context fields, and flashcard backs. Operational knowledge does not require document navigation citations.

Write explanations that teach the controlling condition, exception, or decision rule. When the source provides a reason (e.g., ATD slant-range correction does not match DME readout in 6-4-5), include that reason in the explanation rather than just restating the advisory.

For 6-4-3, ensure items cover the full range of discontinuation conditions (a through d), not only conditions b and c. The passing-NAVAID condition (a) and the RNAV-VOR mixed-equipment condition (d) each represent operationally distinct scenarios.

Avoid generating companion T/F items that restate the same rule in a less demanding format when a multiple-choice or situation_action item already covers the same concept. T/F items in this shard duplicate coverage of the 3-minute condition (6-4-3b), pilot separation conditions (6-4-4), and DME advisory (6-4-5).
Keep minima_rule_check activities self-contained as demonstrated by ddb0aa8f: include lookup_context that establishes the operational conditions and ask for the specific numeric minimum.

Never embed paragraph references (6-5-1, 6-5-2, etc.) in stems, explanations, or activity content fields. Frame questions operationally: 'What heading divergence is required...' not 'Under 6-5-1, what heading divergence...'

Every explanation must teach the controlling principle with at least 15-25 words. For minima questions, explain WHY the value matters (which scenario it applies to, how it relates to the other minima in the table) rather than just restating the number.

True/false questions should be reserved for genuinely binary operational judgments. When a rule states a condition with exceptions, use multiple-choice to test recognition of those conditions rather than T/F rote confirmation.

Ensure activity types are genuinely distinct in cognitive demand. Two activities asking the same factual question in different wrappers (knowledge_check vs minima_rule_check) waste assessment slots and should never appear together.
Situation_action activities should not include para_context fields that repeat the correct answer. The situation alone must provide enough context for the controller to apply the rule.

Flashcard backs covering multi-item rule lists (like the four lateral separation methods) should enumerate each method distinctly rather than conflating them into a summary phrase. If the complete list exceeds 60 words on the back, split into multiple cards.

The DME arc minima paragraph (6-5-3) defines four distinct values (different arcs at 35 or less, different arcs above 35, arc-to-other-airspace at 35 or less, arc-to-other-airspace above 35) — future generation passes should ensure all four are covered, ideally with at least one item each.
The DME slant-range error note (altitudes 3,000 feet or less above NAVAID elevation allow non-DME table values) is entirely uncovered in this shard and should be addressed in a future pass.
Do not include paragraph numbers (e.g., 'Under 6-5-4a4') in question stems. Embed the operational context (aircraft type, airspace, route characteristics) instead.
Write question explanations that state the operational rule directly rather than citing 'Per paragraph X.Y.Z, the answer is...'.
Continue generating situation_action activities with self-contained situation text — the current activity templates are already doing this well.
The minima_rule_check and situation_action activity types are effectively used for these paragraphs; continue pairing them with knowledge_check items for cross-format reinforcement.
Retain exact minima distances (4, 9, 15, 500 miles, 51 miles, 130 miles) as precise retrieval targets where the source prescribes them.
Prohibit paragraph-location references in all stems, explanations, activity prompts, para_context fields, and flashcard fronts. Use self-contained operational language.
Require explanations to restate the controlling rule, condition, or exception without citing a paragraph number. The source is the procedure, not the document location.
Balance choice length across all options in multiple-choice items. When a correct answer combines multiple conditions, ensure at least one distractor is comparably compound.
Avoid negative-stem true/false items for paragraphs where the skill is knowing the positive operational conditions. Reserve true/false for testing individual factual propositions within the rule.
Avoid verbatim source restatements in requirement_check activities. Test a non-trivial operational proposition that requires applying the rule, not recognizing the source text.

Split multi-condition rules (e.g., 6-7-1 single missed approach: course reversal AND weather minima) so that one item does not carry all conditions in a single correct answer. Each condition can be tested independently as a list_membership or knowledge_check item.
Never begin a stem with 'Under 6-X-Y' or embed paragraph cross-references like 'as required by 6-7-1c' — describe the operational condition in plain terms.
Never write an explanation that says 'Per 6-X-Y' or 'That requirement appears in 6-X-Y' — always teach what the rule is and when it applies.
For conditional rules (6-7-4), always include the condition in the stem or question context; do not present conditional requirements as universal.
Avoid creating identical stems and identical choices across question and activity formats — different formats should exercise different retrieval modes.
When writing distractors for situation_action activities, craft operationally plausible mistakes a controller might actually make.

For timed-approach items, reference 'the succeeding aircraft' or 'the second aircraft' consistently and describe fixes as 'the final approach fix, outer marker, or fix used in lieu of the outer marker' when the rule applies to all three.
Do not embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or para_context fields. State the operational rule directly.
Explanations must teach the controlling principle, condition, or exception — not merely identify where in the source the answer can be found.

Do not convert conditional or concurrent procedural actions into ordering/sequencing items. If a paragraph describes two simultaneous duties, test them as a coordinated response, not a numbered sequence.
When an item has a companion in another format (e.g., question + knowledge_check), ensure the retrieval mode is genuinely different. Do not duplicate the exact stem and choices.
Flashcard backs must stay within the scope of the front's question. Do not append actions from a different operational phase.
Keep correct-answer text length comparable to distractor length. A much longer correct answer telegraphs itself.
Distractors must represent plausible operational mistakes, not nonsensical actions like 'tower frequency change regardless of weather' when the paragraph concerns approach procedures.
shard_overviews: The shard covers four short paragraphs (6-1-1 through 6-1-4) with 21 total items across questions, activities, and flashcards. Content fidelity to the source blocks is generally good — no items contain incorrect answers or fabricated source statements. The primary defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: most question stems, explanations, and activity para_context fields embed paragraph numbers ('Under 6-1-1,' 'Per 6-1-4b1,' etc.) that are irrelevant to the operational task. Explanations frequently restate an answer or reference a paragraph number instead of teaching the controlling operational principle. Four T/F+MC question pairs duplicate the same retrieval target with no added learning value. The 6-1-1 paragraph (a single sentence) carried seven items, including four activities of which three tested the same rule statement in minimally different formats. The 6-1-4 items covering the wake turbulence table are the shard's strongest content, combining memory-recall questions, a responsibility-focused knowledge check, a situation-action scenario, and a figure-interpretation activity across distinct retrieval modes.

The shard covers three well-defined source paragraphs with factual accuracy on all minima values. The primary defect across seventeen of twenty-one items is paragraph-location scaffolding: stems, explanations, flashcard fronts, and activity para-context fields embed paragraph numbers that substitute document navigation for operational recall. Two true/false items use long verbatim source quotations that make the correct answer obvious. One knowledge-check activity has a pronounced answer-length cue where the correct choice is substantially longer than distractors. One figure-interpretation activity tests what the figure depicts rather than requiring a lookup decision. Four items are clean and require no intervention. No source-fidelity errors were found; all minima values match the source blocks.

The shard covers three paragraphs (6-3-1, 6-4-1, 6-4-2) with 22 total items across questions, activities, and flashcards. Content fidelity to the source blocks is correct — no items contain factually wrong answers or fabricated source statements. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 18 of 22 items embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, para_context fields, or flashcard fronts. Explanations frequently restate an answer or cite a paragraph number instead of teaching the controlling operational principle. The four flag-free items (two activities, two flashcards) are the shard's best content: self-contained, operationally focused, and well-structured. No items required removal or splitting; all 18 interventions are scaffolding and explanation-quality replacements.

The shard covers three paragraphs (6-4-3, 6-4-4, 6-4-5) with 22 total items across questions, activities, and flashcards. Content fidelity to the source blocks is correct — no items contain factually wrong answers or fabricated source statements. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 18 of 22 items embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, para_context fields, or flashcard backs. Explanations frequently restate an answer or cite a paragraph number instead of teaching the controlling operational principle. Three items are thin T/F restatements that would benefit from more active stems. The four flag-free items (one minima_rule_check activity, two flashcards, and one readback_check activity with only explanation-level flagging) have solid operational content. No items required removal or splitting; all 18 interventions are scaffolding and explanation-quality replacements.

Shard 5 covers three lateral-separation paragraphs (6-5-1, 6-5-2, 6-5-3) with 22 items across three formats. Content fidelity to source is high — every answer is factually correct and no unsafe operational implications were found. The primary defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 16 of 22 items embed 'Under 6-5-X' or 'Per 6-5-X' in stems, explanations, or para_context fields, substituting document trivia for operational framing. Three true/false questions reduce operationally significant rules to verbatim restatements without testing understanding. One activity functionally duplicates another. Two flashcards have imprecise backs. The flashcard and table-lookup items are the strongest formats in this shard.

All nine questions embed paragraph-location scaffolding in their stems (e.g., 'Under 6-5-4a4' or 'Under 6-5-5'), which teaches paragraph-number recall instead of operational decision-making. The answers are substantively correct, and the activities and flashcards are well-formed and self-contained. No items require removal or splitting. The only intervention needed is stripping paragraph references from question stems and tightening explanations to teach the operational rule directly.

All eight questions, seven of eight activities, and one of three flashcards carry paragraph-location scaffolding in stems, explanations, or para_context fields. One question suffers from answer-length cue that reveals the correct choice. One question uses a negative-stem true/false format that tests exclusion recognition rather than the positive operational conditions. Two explanations are so thin they amount to paragraph citations without any instructional content. No items are factually wrong or require removal. The source paragraphs are well-bounded and the factual coverage is accurate; remediation is confined to removing paragraph-location scaffolding, strengthening thin explanations, converting one negative-stem item to a positive-stem multiple-choice question, and balancing answer choice length.

This shard covers four related timed-approach paragraphs (6-7-2 through 6-7-5). The content is source-accurate but suffers from pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding in stems and explanations, several thin explanations that merely point to a paragraph number instead of teaching the rule, a handful of missing-conditions source-fidelity issues in 6-7-4 items, one near-duplicate across formats, and one activity with an absurd distractor. Of 24 total items, 18 require replacement, 1 requires removal, and 5 are clean. The paragraph 6-7-4 items universally omitted the conditional trigger 'when 6-7-2b is applied and the weather report indicates IFR conditions over the fix,' presenting a conditional rule as an unconditional requirement.

Two-paragraph shard with 11 items covering timed approach time checks and missed approach procedures. Source fidelity is sound in every item — no incorrect operational statements. However, ten of eleven items have at least one defect. Paragraph-location scaffolding pervades stems, explanations, and para_context fields, teaching document structure instead of ATC substance. Two items (an ordering question and a sequence_steps activity) misrepresent concurrent procedural actions as a linear sequence — the ordering answer key is invalid because post-miss actions happen together, not in order. One knowledge_check is an exact duplicate of its companion multiple-choice question. The 6-7-7 flashcard conflates pre-miss and post-miss actions in a single back field. Explanation quality is the most consistent weakness: several explanations are vacuous paragraph pointers or thin restatements that do not teach the operational rule.

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under 6-1-1,' embedding paragraph-number scaffolding that is irrelevant to the operational decision. The explanation restates the answer without teaching the controlling operational principle.
operations.source_basis: 6-1-1: 'Use mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima only when direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications are maintained.'

match.question_text: Under 6-1-1, when may mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima be used?

replacements.question_text: When may mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima be used?
replacements.explanation: Mileage-based procedures and minima require direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications. Relayed communications, CPDLC, or other indirect contact methods do not satisfy the requirement.

Whenever radar contact exists, even if pilot/controller communications are relayed.
Whenever an aircraft has DME, regardless of the communications method in use.
Only after a pilot requests mileage-based separation.

replacements.question_text: When a position report affecting separation is not received, when must action be taken to obtain the report?
replacements.explanation: The 5-minute deadline begins from the time the aircraft was estimated to cross the fix, not from when the controller notices the report is missing. Action must be initiated before that 5-minute window closes.

replacements.explanation: The DME arc separation minima between different arcs about a NAVAID apply regardless of direction of flight. Whether the aircraft are flying clockwise, counterclockwise, in the same direction, or opposite directions on their respective arcs does not change the applicable minimum. The controller applies the same minimum based on distance from the NAVAID.

choices.text: Yes, the DME arc minimum applies regardless of direction of flight.
No, opposite-direction arcs require 20 miles regardless of distance from the NAVAID.
Yes, but only when both arcs are at 35 miles or less from the NAVAID.
No, the aircraft must be flying the same direction for the arc minimum to apply.

content.explanation: One approved lateral-separation method is to clear aircraft on different airways or routes whose widths or protected airspace do not overlap. The controller uses this method by assigning each aircraft to a route whose protected airspace is separate and non-overlapping with the other aircraft's route. Other approved methods include using specified diverging headings, different geographical locations, and separate holding fixes.
